coredata.dc:title: Temperature, strain-rate and charge dependent mechanical response of Li-ion pouch cells

coredata.dc:creator: Tancogne-Dejean, Thomas
Hayoun, Gabriel
Mohr, Dirk
coredata.dc:description: A combined experimental-numerical investigation is carried out on the mechanical response of commercial 4 Ah Li-ion pouch cells. Hemispherical indentations were performed across a broad range of conditions, including temperatures from –5 °C to 70 °C, strain rates from 0.001 s⁻¹ to 1000 s⁻¹, and states of charge (SoC) from 0.0 to 0.97. The experiments demonstrate that temperature and strain rate are the dominant factors influencing cell behavior, with higher temperatures leading to pronounced softening (43 % reduction in maximum force from 20 °C to 70 °C). The strain rate dependency is non-monotonic, with a 50 %-reduction in short-circuit displacement from slow to high speed and a 15 % reduction in maximum force from slow to intermediate speed. The influence of the SoC on the mechanical response is comparatively minor. To capture all these effects, a phenomenological constitutive model is proposed. It employs a pressure-sensitive Deshpande–Fleck yield surface with associated plastic flow and incorporates a convex-to-linear hardening transition modulated by temperature and SoC. Strain rate and softening effects are described in close analogy with Johnson–Cook-type of models. Finite element simulations performed with the identified surrogate model showed good agreement across all indentation experiments. The proposed model provides a robust framework for predicting the mechanical behavior of Li-ion cells under varying conditions, offering immediate applicability for the design of safer battery systems in electronic devices and vehicles.

head.ce:title: Temperature, strain-rate and charge dependent mechanical response of Li-ion pouch cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A combined experimental-numerical investigation is carried out on the mechanical response of commercial 4 Ah Li-ion pouch cells. Hemispherical indentations were performed across a broad range of conditions, including temperatures from –5 °C to 70 °C, strain rates from 0.001 s⁻¹ to 1000 s⁻¹, and states of charge (SoC) from 0.0 to 0.97. The experiments demonstrate that temperature and strain rate are the dominant factors influencing cell behavior, with higher temperatures leading to pronounced softening (43 % reduction in maximum force from 20 °C to 70 °C). The strain rate dependency is non-monotonic, with a 50 %-reduction in short-circuit displacement from slow to high speed and a 15 % reduction in maximum force from slow to intermediate speed. The influence of the SoC on the mechanical response is comparatively minor. To capture all these effects, a phenomenological constitutive model is proposed. It employs a pressure-sensitive Deshpande–Fleck yield surface with associated plastic flow and incorporates a convex-to-linear hardening transition modulated by temperature and SoC. Strain rate and softening effects are described in close analogy with Johnson–Cook-type of models. Finite element simulations performed with the identified surrogate model showed good agreement across all indentation experiments. The proposed model provides a robust framework for predicting the mechanical behavior of Li-ion cells under varying conditions, offering immediate applicability for the design of safer battery systems in electronic devices and vehicles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries in transportation industry and energy storage is increasing with the transition away from the use of fossil fuels. Sales of electric vehicles have soared and this will continue over the next few decades [1–3]. Li-ion cells are made of a stacking of anode, polymeric separator, and cathode, soaked with an electrolyte solution allowing for the flow of ions between electrodes. Battery manufacturers constantly improve the gravimetric and volumetric energy densities of cells. To maintain or improve the safety of electronic devices, including the crashworthiness of electric vehicles, understanding the mechanical behavior of the jelly-stack is paramount. In addition, accurate surrogate models for batteries are essential to enable engineers to optimize the design of protective structures around batteries. The critical load cases include battery indentation, which can cause short-circuit and thermal runaway [4].

Environmental conditions such as temperature and loading speed along with the cell’s state, as its state of charge (SoC) or the state of health influence the mechanical response. Gilaki and Sahraei [5] and Mo et al. [6] studied the effect of temperature on the mechanical response of LFP/graphite and NMC/graphite cylindrical cells, respectively. Wang and Xia [7] performed indentation experiments on pouch cells of NMC/graphite chemistry. They all reported a significant softening (up to 50 % of the maximum force within cells operating conditions) in the effective stress-strain response. By performing tensile, compression and indentation experiments at the anode and cathode level, Wang and Xia [7] attributed this effect to the softening of the polymeric binder present on the anodes and cathodes, while Gilaki and Sahraei reported a softening of the polymeric separator. The effect of strain rate on the indentation response has been experimentally investigated in a large number of studies (e.g. [8,9]). Kisters and co-workers reported a significant increase in the cell stiffness with increasing strain rate; they also observed a decrease in the displacement at the onset of short circuit for various elliptical, pouch and prismatic cells [10–12]. They attributed the strain rate effect to the motion of the liquid electrolyte (or its absence) in the porous granular media [13,14].

During charging, the cathode expands by 1.8 %, while the anode expands by 5.2 % [15]. This swelling leads to the build-up of internal stresses, ultimately creating significant forces under confined conditions (e.g. [16,17]). Li et al. [18] performed experiments on charged cylindrical and pouch cells. They observed a stiffening of the cylindrical cells and a softening of the pouch cells. They attributed this behavior to the internal stresses generated by charging-induced swelling. Besides swelling, the mechanical properties of the anode (and moderately the one of the cathode) are modified with the state of charge (e.g. [19–22]). Wang et al. [23] investigated the coupled effect of strain rate and state of charge for NMC/graphite pouch cells undergoing hemispherical indentation at loading speeds ranging from 1mm/min to 500mm/min. They reported a softening of the cell with increasing state of charge across all speeds. Another parameter influencing the mechanical response is the state of health (e.g. [24–26]). The state of health typically correlates with a softening of the cells and a delayed increased in the force.

To enable numerical simulations of Li-ion cells, homogenized and detailed constitutive models have been developed [4]. The detailed models treat the cell as a layered structure, separately representing every component [8,27–29]. This can be very effective to capture the complex kinematics occurring during in-plane loading as well as bending [30–32]. Furthermore, this can inform macroscopic plasticity and failure models through homogenization [8,33–38]. However, those models require accurate modeling of each of the constituent materials (e.g. [39–43]) as well as their interfaces (e.g. [44]). The enclosure of the cell, whether cylindrical, pouch or prismatic should also be modeled. Zhang et al. [45] characterized the plasticity and failure of the shell casing of a 18650 cylindrical cell using curved tensile specimen. Tancogne-Dejean et al. [46] made use of miniature tensile and bulge specimen to characterize the plasticity and failure of the Aluminum casing of a large format prismatic cell and reveal location dependent material response. Similar results are obtained by Cioni et al. [47] and Sahraei et al. [48]. In the case of pouch cells, the non-symmetric combination of aluminum and various polymers leads to very large anisotropy of the materials as well as viscous effects [49–51]. The homogenized models focus on developing efficient model at the cell level (e.g. [9,44,52–61]). Amongst others, those models rely on the LS-Dyna based MAT_HONEYCOMB, the Deshpande-Fleck yield surface [62], the Drucker-Prager cap model [63], critical state plasticity or anisotropic models such as the Raghava model [64]. They also include the effect of strain rate and state of charge through modification of the flow stress or yield surface. Ying et al. [39] incorporated the temperature dependency of the anode modifying the cohesion in a Drucker-Prager cap model, while Li and Zhu [59] modified the hardening response of the cell to include anisotropy. Kisters et al. [13] incorporated the strain rate effect using the Mat_Add_Permeability from LS-Dyna. Tancogne-Dejean et al. [65] performed experiments on large-format pouch cell and proposed a strain-rate dependent hardening model capturing the mechanical response of across six decades of strain rate. The model incorporates the strain rate hardening of the initial yield stress and the subsequent hardening and describe the softening observed at intermediate strain rates (∼1/s) using a plastic work dependent softening. This model was applied successfully on the uniaxial compression of small pouch cells by Cioni et al. [66]. The low computational cost of the homogenized model allow for their use in the study of battery modules [67–70] and even electric vehicle crashworthiness (e.g. [71–74]). In these situations, the cells will feature any state of charge (discharged to charged), strain rates would lie between 1/s and 1000/s, while the temperature would typically be between 0 °C and 60 °C, requiring the development of a temperature, strain-rate and state of charge dependent model.

In the present work, we develop a phenomenological surrogate model for Li-ion jelly stacks capable of predicting the large-deformation response of battery cells as a function of temperature, strain rate, and state of charge. Section 2 details the experimental procedures, including a novel temperature-control setup based on a recirculating chiller. The constitutive framework, with an extended hardening law to capture temperature and state-of-charge effects, is presented in Section 3. Section 4 reports hemispherical indentation experiments on small-format (4 Ah) commercial Li-ion pouch cells across varying thermal, mechanical, and electrochemical conditions, while Section 5 analyzes the corresponding finite element simulations. A discussion of the combined findings is provided in Section 6, with conclusions summarized in Section 7. The key experimental observation is that the macroscopic mechanical response of Li-ion jelly stacks is strongly coupled to all three factors—temperature, strain rate, and state of charge. Building on this, we propose the first homogenized constitutive model that incorporates these dependencies.

Indentations experiments are performed on small format 4Ah pouch cells across various strain rates, temperature and state of charge. We first present the inner architecture of the cells and describe the experimental methodology including a newly developed temperature control system and the Split Hopkinson pressure bars used in high strain rate experiments. Then, the definition of initial displacement and onset of short circuit are provided.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material: lithium-ion cells with defined state of charge
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental campaign is performed on commercially available 4.0 Ah (15.44 Wh) pouch cells with Lithium Cobalt Oxide (LCO) / graphite chemistry. In the fully discharge state, the cell dimensions are 65.5 mm x 58.6 mm in-plane, with a thickness of 5.07 mm. Both cell terminals are positioned on one of the short sides of the cell (see Fig. 1 a-b). The inner architecture of the cell is composed of 34 unit-cells, i.e. 34 layers of separator, 17 double-sided anode layers (graphite coated on a copper current collector), 16 double-sided cathodes (active material coated on aluminum current collector) and two single-sided cathode layers at both ends of the jelly-stack. Their respective thicknesses are 0.145 mm for the anode, 0.120 mm for cathode and 0.022 mm for the separator. The jelly-stack is enclosed in a pouch made of a 0.10 mm thick polymer-aluminum composite (see Fig 1d), with an average roughness below 0.2 µm. Scanning Electron Microscope images of each layer can be seen in [75].

All experiments are performed on cells undergoing their first commercial discharge cycle. Prior to testing, all cells are fully discharged using a constant current constant voltage (CCCV) strategy with a discharging current of 2.0A (i.e. 0.5C). Discharge is first performed at constant current (and decreasing tension) up to a target cutoff voltage of 3.0V where the discharge is then continued at constant tension (and decreasing current) down to 0.2A. For selected cells, a charging step is also carried out up to a target State of Charge (SoC) using a CCCV strategy with a charging current of 2.0A and a cutoff voltage of 4.43V. The charging protocol ends either upon reaching the desired SoC or upon reaching a current of 0.2Ain the constant voltage part (full charging). It should be noted that the state of charge of the cell is defined, using the Coulomb counting method, as the ratio of the added capacity to the nominal capacity of the cell (here 4.0Ah) starting from the fully discharged state. The state of charge varies from χ C = 0 (fully discharged cell) to χ C = 1 (fully charged cell). The thickness of each cell is measured after the charging process to consider the charged-induced swelling and scales from 5.07 mm to 5.27 mm at χ C = 0.97. 1

Indentation experiments are performed on cells at various states of charge and temperature. Batteries are indented in the center of the largest sides (out-of-plane loading, Fig. 1a) with a hemispherical punch, with the indenter motion parallel to the thickness direction. The loading is carried out until mechanical failure or onset of fume release/fire. Prior to each experiment, the indenter (with an average roughness below 0.4 µm) is lubricated with grease (Isoflex NBU 15, Kluber Lubrification). To assess repeatability, two experiments are performed for each state of charge/loading speed combination.

Two sets of experiments are carried out. Temperature dependent indentations are performed on discharged cells (χ C = 0) at constant speed corresponding to an average engineering strain rate (loading speed over initial cell height) of 10−3/s. The indenter has a radius of 10 mm and the temperature ranges from −5 °C to 70 °C. State of charge dependent experiments make use of a 15mm-radius indenter. The experiments are performed at three loading speeds, corresponding to an average engineering strain rate of 10−3/s, 1/s and 103/s (denoted later as slow, intermediate and high speeds). The temperature is kept at 20 °C and the range of states of charge is between χ C = 0 (fully discharged) and χ C = 1 (fully charged).

For safety reasons, all experiments are carried out in custom-made steel enclosures (see for example Fig. 2 b) connected to a gas cleaning device (Fig. 2c). The gas cleaning device is composed of a tank filled with 1 m3 of activated charcoal (part of it being alkali-treated for acid gases cleaning) connected to a fan with 300 m3/h flow. A glass window is added to the safety enclosure to enable optical displacement monitoring (Fig. 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Slow and intermediate loading velocities with temperature control
ce:section.ce:para: Cell indentation at slow and intermediate velocities are performed on a 250 kN electromechanical universal testing machine (Instron 5580), equipped with a protective enclosure of dimensions 500 mm x 500 mm x 500 mm (Fig. 2a-b). The cells ① are placed on a flat steel platen ②. To control the temperature of the cell, the platen and indenter ③ are kept at the desired temperature through the circulation ④ of water-glycol mixture using a recirculating chiller (Accel 500LT, ThermoFisher). The cell temperature is adjusted by conduction and monitored using a thermocouple (type K). The platen is separated from the bottom of the testing machine with a low thermal conductivity ceramic plate. The load cell is kept at room temperature with a circulation of room temperature water at the end of the indenter (⑤, 400 mm away from the controlled temperature liquid).

All slow experiments are performed at a speed of 0.3 mm/min. The discharged, temperature-dependent experiments are performed at six different temperatures, i.e. {−5, 0., 20, 40, 60, 70} °C, while the room temperature, SoC-dependent low speed experiments are performed for six different states of charge, i.e. {0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.88, 0.95}. The intermediate speed experiments are performed at a speed of 3000 mm/min for three states of charge, i.e. {0, 0.5, 0.97} at room temperature.

For all experiments, images are acquired to monitor the displacements of the moving indenter, the platen and the side of the cell. The force and open-circuit cell voltage are acquired synchronously. For the slow indentation, images are acquired with 12MPx camera (PointGrey GS3-U3–120S6C-M, FLIR) equipped with a 60 mm lens at a frequency of 1 Hz. A high-speed camera (FastCam SA-Z, Photron) is used to acquire the images at an acquisition frequency of 250 Hz. Table 1 provides a summary of the most relevant DIC hardware and software parameters.

For room-temperature high strain rate experiments, a dedicated Split Hopkinson Pressure Bar setup is set-up (Fig. 3 ). Aluminum alloy (AA7075) bars of 30 mm diameter are used. The striker, input and output bar lengths are respectively 1.2 m, 2.9 m and 1.8 m. A 0.195 m long aluminum extension with a 30 mm diameter hemispherical end is added between the input bar and the specimen. Strain gages are positioned at a distance of 1.45 m from the interface between the input bar and the extension and 0.2 m from the output bar / cell interface. The system dimensions allow for an experimental duration of 0.46 ms. The cell, extension and output bar end are enclosed in a safety enclosure with dimensions 280 mm x 300 mm x 500 mm. The experiments are performed for three states of charge, i.e. {0, 0.5, 0.93}. For all experiments, the initial striker speed is about 7.1 m/s. For the post-processing, dispersion correction for the wave transport is used through the software DAVID [76]. After verification of the equilibrium, the force is computed using the transmitted signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Definition of initial displacement and onset of short circuit
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to the initial imperfections of the cell surface (e.g. adhesive holding the jellyrolls, dents, etc.) as well as the very small slope of the initial part of the force-displacement curve, it is difficult to find the initial point of contact between the cell and the indenter (i.e. the displacement origin for the mechanical indentation). We define the displacement origin using an analytical approximation, following [75]. For this, a power law is fitted to the beginning of the force-displacement curve using the formula,

.
For high strain rate experiments (where the bars hold the cell specimen), the initial displacement is defined as the last displacement where zero force is measured.

Two critical values of the open circuit voltage are defined for further discussion; the first voltage drop and the short circuit initiation voltage. The first voltage drop is defined as the first instant where the voltage falls below the mean of the voltage minus five standard deviations. For slow and intermediate velocities, both mean and standard deviation are computed during the first part of the indentation, i.e. up to 10 % of the maximum force. For high strain rate experiments, the mean and the standard deviation are computed from the voltage recorded prior to the loading of the cell. For all experiments, the standard deviation is less than 6.7 × 10−4 V. The short circuit initiation voltage is defined as the first instant at which the slope of the voltage with respect to the displacement (computed through finite difference) falls below −0.7 V/mm.

The jellystack (Fig. 1d) is the main building block (unit cell) of the periodic microstructure of Li-ion cells. A surrogate model is developed that describes the macroscopic stress-strain response of the jellystack, which can then be used in structural models representing full Li-ion cells. Due to its small thickness in comparison with the jellystack, the pouch is not explicitly modeled. The starting point is the rate-dependent elasto-plastic model of Tancogne-Dejean et al. [65]. Here, we briefly recall the model formulation and extend it to capture the effects of temperature and state of charge. The homogenized elastic response is described by the Poisson’s ratio ν and the Young’s modulus E.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material model parameter identification from indentation experiments
ce:section.ce:para:
The elastic constants {E, ν}, the plastic Poisson’s ratio {ν p } and the rate-independent hardening parameters
{
k 0
, A , n ,
ε
c 0
, Q , B
}
have been identified previously from static experiments [75]. Here, we outline a hybrid experimental-numerical inverse method to identify the material model parameters that characterize the effects of strain rate
{
ε
˙
1
, D ,
ε
˙
2
, C
}
, softening {η, m}, temperature {CT,Tr } and the effect of charge {C χ}. The inverse method is based on finite element simulations of indentation experiments on battery cells. It targets the minimization of the mean relative error (MRE) between the simulated and experimentally-measured force-displacement curve.

The finite element simulations are performed using the software Abaqus/explicit. A quarter cell model is built taking advantage of the symmetry of the problem (omitting the electrical connectors). The mesh is composed of first-order hexahedral elements with reduced integration (C3D8R in Abaqus) with 35 elements through the cell thickness and an in-plane element size of 0.18 mm in the critical region (see Fig. 1c). This mesh is identical to the one used in [75]. As the indenters used in the present study are much larger, it should be noted that a mesh size of 0.25 mm (20 elements through thickness) would result in converged simulations.

The hemispherical indenter and the baseplate are modeled as analytical rigid surfaces. During indentation, the indenter is moving vertically downwards (with the other degrees of freedom fixed) while the baseplate is fixed. The contact interaction is modeled with a kinematic contact and a friction coefficient of 0.1. It should be noted that the friction coefficient has a minor influence on the force displacement response with less than 2.5 % discrepancies upon changing the friction coefficient between 0.0 and 0.2. For the slow and intermediate velocity simulations, mass scaling is used to reduce the computational time while ensuring quasi-static loading conditions, with a minimum of 200′000 increments. No self-heating of the specimen is considered.

The identification is done in three steps:
(i)
identification of the strain-rate dependent hardening (and softening) parameters
{
C , D ,
ε
˙
1
, η , m
}
using the intermediate and fast experiments on fully-discharged batteries (χ C = 0),
(ii)
identification of the temperature dependent parameter {CT } based on all temperature experiments, and
(iii)
identification of the parameter {C χ} based on the low-speed experiments with varying state of charge.

The minimization is performed starting at a displacement of 0.25 mm, up to the first instant where the measured force reaches 90 % of the maximum force, which roughly corresponds to the onset of a concave force-displacement response. Cost functions are minimized with a gradient-free Nelder-Mead algorithm implemented in SciPy [78], followed by a final manual parameter tuning step. The remaining strain rate and SoC experiments are used for the validation of the identified model.

This section starts by presenting the typical result of an out-of-plane hemispherical indentation on a lithium-ion pouch. The results of the experimental campaign with various temperatures are the presented along with the strain-rate and state of charge dependent experiments.

The main outcome of an out-of-plane indentation experiment on a lithium-ion cell is the force-displacement curve of the punch, the associated voltage-displacement curve and the cell displacement versus punch displacement curve. Fig. 4 a shows a typical example of these measurements for indentations with a 10 mm radius punch at slow speed and at 20 °C. The main features of a force-displacement curve are an initially convex force evolution, here up to a displacement of about 1 mm, followed by a linear evolution with a slope of 6.35 kN/mm and a transition a concave evolution before mechanical failure. The maximum force is 6.95 kN attained at a displacement of 1.63 mm. The transition from convex to linear force evolution is associated with a vertical displacement (lift up) of the cell away from the indenter (grey curve), at a rate of 1.7 mm/mm. The voltage remains constant up to the first voltage drop (diamond symbol, 1.60 mm) followed by a large voltage drop corresponding to the onset of short circuit (open circle, 1.62 mm), almost coincident with the maximum force. Selected side views of the experiments at the indenter displacements of 0.4 mm, 0.8 mm, 1.2 mm and 1.6 mm are shown on Fig. 4b with the displacement field on the cells. For all steps, the side motion is almost rigid with less than 0.05 mm variation between the minimum and maximum displacement. Initially, the cell slightly moves downward by about 0.1 mm to accommodate slight rotation and non-uniform thickness of the cell. Then starting at 1 mm of indentation, the cell lift-up in a rigid body motion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature dependent indentation response (20 mm diameter punch)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 summarizes the results from indentation experiments on fully-discharged batteries for temperatures ranging from −5 to 70 ºC. The repeatability of the experiments is excellent with differences in force of less than 0.25 kN (for all temperatures), except for the displacement at the onset of short circuit and the maximum force. The temperature strongly influences the mechanical response at large displacements, where an increase in temperature leads to a softer response and earlier mechanical failure. For all temperatures, the force-displacement curves (Fig. 5a) exhibit the transition from the convex to the linear regime at a displacement of about 0.9 ± 0.1mm. At room temperature, we observe a maximum force of 6.96 kN. Similar values are observed for lower temperatures (e.g. 6.77 kN at −5 °C). However, the force maximum decreases monotonically when increasing the temperature, leading to a reduction of 43 % (down to 4.0 kN) when performing the experiment at 70 °C. When plotting the maximum force as a function of the temperature (Fig. 5c), we observe that the effect of temperature on the force vanishes at temperatures below 20 °C and at temperatures above 60 °C. In addition to looking at the effect of temperature on the maximum force, we also computed the slopes of the linear parts of the force-displacement curves (range of 65 % to 85 % of the maximum force). The slope reduces by 23 % from the lowest to the highest temperature (Fig. 5d). The cell lift-up occurs earlier with increasing temperature (from 1.0 mm to 0.9 mm, see Fig. 5a). However, the lift-up rate is not significantly affected by the temperature.

It should be noted that the displacement at first voltage drop (Fig. 5b) is moderately affected by the change in temperature, from a minimum value of 1.35 mm at 70 °C to a maximum value of 1.6 mm at 20 °C. The voltage (Fig. 5b) stays within five standard deviations for a very long part of the curve. For temperatures below 20 °C, the voltage starts decreasing before the maximum force while for the highest temperatures, the first voltage drop occurs coincidentally or even after the maximum force. The rate of voltage drops is also affected with low temperature showing slow voltage drops and high temperature exhibiting rapid decline (see inset Fig. 5b). All values are reported in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: State of charge and strain-rate dependent response (30 mm diameter punch)

ce:section.ce:para: The same two regimes are observed in the 15 mm-radius indentations for all states of charge (see Fig. 6 a). The repeatability of the experiments is excellent with less than 0.3 kN differences between identical states of charge. As for the temperature, the influence of the state of charge is negligible in the initial convex regime of the experiment. However, the instant of convex-linear transition appears to dependent on the SoC; the transition occurs at smaller displacement with an increasing state of charge (from 0.95 mm when fully discharged to 0.82 mm at a SoC of 0.95). Fig. 6d shows the effect of state of charge on the slope in the linear regime. In the slow experiments, the slope decreases with increasing state of charge from 10.5 kN/mm to 9.5 kN/mm. This in turn leads to 13 % lower maximum force (from 12.9 kN to 11. 2 kN, see Fig. 6e) and 11 % lower force at the instant of short circuit. The displacement at the onset of short circuit is almost independent of the state of charge. All results are summarized in Table 3 . The cell lift-up starts earlier with increasing SoC (from 0.95 mm to 0.87 mm). The lift-up rate shows a non-monotonic evolution with a minimum at a state of charge of 0.5 and the highest values observed for fully charged cells.

The open-circuit voltage of the cells remains constant during the indentation until the onset of short-circuit, with a maximum displacement difference of 0.2 mm between the first voltage drop and the onset of short-circuit. Force maximum and subsequent drop follows immediately (see Fig. 6a). Starting at a SoC of 0.5, smoke release and fire are observed (Fig. 6f) after the onset of short circuit.

Fig. 6b shows the force-displacement curves for the indentations at intermediate speed. As for the previous experiments, the variation amongst repetition of the same experiments is less than 0.3 kN up to the onset of short circuit. Contrary to the slow case, the SoC had no noticeable effect in the convex-linear transition in the intermediate speed experiments. This transition occurs earlier and in a less abrupt manner. The curve slope as well as selected displacements and forces reported in Table 4 . The slope slightly decreases from χ C = 0 to χ C = 0.5, before increasing again for the experiments with χ C = 0.96. Overall, the state of charge has little influence on the mechanical response with a maximum variation of 8 % in the force at a given displacement (see Fig. 6b). It should be noted that the force continues to increase between the onset of short circuit and the maximum force for up to 0.4 mm, even for experiments that experience thermal runaway. At intermediate strain rate, the lift-up occurs as early as 0.4 mm and its rate increases with state of charge, from 1.15 mm/mm to 1.32 mm/mm.

The evolution of the open circuit voltage differs from the slow indentation experiments. The voltage remains constant for only several tenths of millimeters (up to 1.4 mm) before decreasing linearly up to the onset of short circuit. The voltage slope decreases (in absolute value) with an increasing state of charge from −0.038 V/mm for discharged cells to about −0.009 V/mm for SoC of 0.96. The onset of short circuit is clearly visible in the voltage curves indicating the occurrence of a sudden event.

At high indentation speed, the initial convex regime is also approximately linear; the convex-to-linear transition primarily defines pronounced increase in slope of the force-displacement curves (Fig. 6c). The displacement at the onset of short circuit reduces from about 1.8 mm at lower loading speeds to 0.92 mm while the maximum force remains constant around 12 kN. The slope computed between 25 % and 85 % of the maximum force vary slightly with state of charge, from 15.1 kN/mm for χ C = 0 to 15.4 kN/mm at χ C = 0.5 to 15.15 kN/mm for χ C = 0.93. It should be noted that those slope values are 49 % and 70 % higher than their slow and intermediate speed counterparts respectively. The maximum force decreases by about 5 % with an increasing state of charge (see cyan colored dots in Fig. 6e).

Contrary to the experiments at slow and intermediate loading velocities, the open-circuit voltage decreases right from the beginning of the loading (about 0.1 mm) up to the onset of short-circuit where a faster drop is observed. The voltage drops regularly at a rate of −0.045V/mm for fully discharged cells and −0.03V/mm for the charged cells (both χ C = 0.5 and χ C = 0.93). Due to the acquisition speed (100 kHz), the onset of short-circuit appears progressive, especially for the discharged cells and can even occur after a large force drop. Similarly, thermal runaway is observed after the maximum force in these experiments. Table 5 summarizes all relevant displacement and forces along with the slope.

In summary, we note that the coupled strain-rate and state of charge dependent experimental campaign reveals that increasing the state of charge changes the tangent stiffness of the cell in the second regime (for slow and intermediate speeds) and reduces the force at the onset of short-circuit. However, the effect of the state of charge becomes less significant as the strain rate increases. The maximum force (Fig. 6e) is reduced between fully discharged and fully charged cells by 13 % for low speed indentations while it is reduced by less than 5 % for the high-speed experiments. Similarly, the slope is reduced by 8 % for low-speed indentation and appears constant for the high-speed indentations.
The calibrated material model is presented in this section. This fully specified model is then used to performed finite element simulations which are compared to the experiments.

A summary of the material model parameters obtained after completing the optimization is shown in Table 6 . Figs. 7 a-b show the strain rate dependent hardening (at room temperature and for fully-discharged cells). The white line represents the deformation resistance at the critical strain ε c . For strain rates of up to 10−2/s, we observe a positive strain rate effect on the deformation resistance. Softening then seems to dominate up to a strain rate of 10−1/s, near
ε
˙
1
= 0.2 / s
, before the strain rate effect returns to being positive. A plateau stress similar to the slowest strain rate is achieved at a strain rate of 1.4/s. The strain rate dependent initial yield strongly affects the deformation resistance with values up to 115 MPa at initial yield for a strain rate of 103/s. It should be noted that the softening leads to lower strain hardening up to the stress plateau, from 91 MPa at 10−3/s, to 67 MPa at 1/s to only 45 MPa at 103/s. Fig. 7b shows deformation resistance equivalent plastic strain curves at an equivalent plastic strain rate of 10−3/s, 1/s and 103/s. Fig. 7c-d shows the temperature dependent response for discharged cells at a strain rate of 10−3/s, and Fig. 7d shows the deformation resistance evolution from temperature of 0 °C, 20 °C, 40 °C and 60 °C. The temperature modifies the convex to linear transition and thus affects the stress value in the linear regime from 93.2 MPa at 0 °C to 88 MPa at 60 °C, at a rate of 0.086 MPa/ °C as discussed in Eq. (17).

The influence of the model parameters
{
A ,
ε
c 0
, B
}
on the low-speed indentation response (10mm-radius at room temperature or for discharged cells) is shown on Fig. 8 . The parameters are changed by 10 % and 25 % around the base values reported in Table 6.
•
The parameter A changes the initial slope of the hardening curves, which in turn changes the equivalent stress at the transition strain (Fig. 8a). Increasing A affects the global force-displacement response in two ways: (i) it augments the rate of increase in the convex regime, and (ii) it increases the slope in the linear regime (Fig. 8b).
•
The parameter
ε
c 0
changes the transition strain between the convex and linear hardening regime and thus the equivalent stress in the second hardening regime (Fig. 8c). An increase in
ε
c 0
thus leads to a delayed transition in the force-displacement curve as well as a higher slope in the subsequent linear part (Fig. 8d).
•
Finally, an increase slope of the linear hardening B (Fig. 8e) solely influences the linear slope in the force-displacement curve (Fig. 8f). It should be noted that large variations of this parameter (up to 10 times) are required to affect the global force-displacement response.

Among the effects of the parameters that are not explicitly shown in Fig. 8, we note that the parameter C controls the strain-rate dependency of the subsequent hardening, while the parameters {η, m} control the plastic work related softening observed at intermediate speeds. The initial yield stress increase at high strain rate is governed by the strain-rate hardening factor D.

The effect of the change in thickness due to charging is shown in Fig. 9 . Two simulations with a 15 mm radius indenter at low speed and room temperature are performed with the thickness change corresponding to a state of charge of 0.0 and 0.97, i.e. 5.07 mm and 5.27 mm. The indentation response is barely affected by the thickness change with a maximum difference of 0.041 kN respectively.

Fig. 10 shows the simulation results for the temperature dependent 10mm-radius indentations. All curves have been shifted by 0.4 kN to increase the readability. The model is able to accurately represent the slow speed room temperature indentation even upon adding the strain rate dependency. Incorporating the temperature dependency of the transition strain allows to accurately represent the temperature induced softening. For all temperatures, the maximum error is less than 10 %, observed in the final concave part of the curve. Comparing the predicted slope to the experimental one, a maximum deviation of less than 1 % is observed for the temperature between 0 °C and 60 °C and below 9 % for the extreme cases of −5 °C and 70 °C. This is tentatively attributed to a saturation of the effects of temperature at extreme temperatures.

Fig. 11 presents the simulation results for the strain-rate and state of charge dependent 15mm-radius indentations. Using the base model (gray curve in Fig. 11a), the numerical simulation underestimates the experimental slope at low speed by 11 %, together with a force error of at the onset of short circuit of 2.8 %. In order to better calibrate the state of charge effect, the base model is adjusted by changing the value of the transition strain from 0.18 to 0.187 (black line on Fig. 11a). With this modification, the model deviates from the experiment by less than 4.5 % at slow speed together with a force error of at the onset of short circuit of 1 %. The simulations agree well with the experiments at intermediate and high speeds with deviation in the slope of 1.8 % and 1.5 % respectively. It should be noted that the change in transition strain barely changes the intermediate and high-speed response. This is due to the large value of the parameter D and reproduces the experimentally observed behavior. Fig. 11 (b-f) shows the state of charge dependent response at all strain rates. Adding the state of charge dependency, the transition strain ranges from 0.187 (χ C = 0) to 0.179 (χ C = 0.97). As for the temperature dependency, adding a linear evolution of the transition strain with the state of charge allows for the accurate modeling of the state of charge dependent response from fully discharged to fully charged cells. At low speed, the maximum error is 1 kN at the onset of short circuit. It should be noted that the error is increasing with the state of charge. On the other hand, the maximum error on the slope is 8 %. At intermediate speed, a slight offset of the response for the fully charged cell is observed, leading to a 0.4 kN offset. Finally, the high-speed simulations accurately capture the experimental responses with an identical slope (between 25 % and 85 % of the maximum force) of 15.3 kN/mm for all states of charge, compared to an average experimental value of 15.2 kN/mm.

In this section, we discuss the observed effects and some of their origins. The potential effect of self-heating is also considered. The effect of the temperature, strain rate and state of charge on the open circuit voltage evolution and potential damage mechanisms are addressed.

The temperature greatly influences the mechanical response of pouch cells. Firstly, the temperature influences the second (linear) regime of the force displacement curves in indentation experiments, characterized by a decreasing slope (about 20 %) with increasing temperature. Secondly, the force and displacement at the instant of first voltage drop decreases with increasing temperature. The force decreases by almost 50 %, while the displacement decreases by 15 % from room temperature to 70 °C. This effect is slightly more pronounced than the one observed in [7], who reported a decrease in the peak force of 50 % from −30 °C to 80 °C for a NMC/graphite pouch cell. Similar to the present findings, the temperature mostly affects the large strain response (i.e. displacement above 20 % of the cell thickness). Conversely for cylindrical cells undergoing cylindrical indentation, the temperature affects the mechanical response right from the beginning. Across all temperatures a maximum in force and displacement is observed at 20 °C. Based on individual component (anode and cathode) experiments, Wang and Xia attributed this thermal softening to the softening of the binder holding the anode and cathode particles [7,39]. Accurately capturing this response is critical as the operating temperature of lithium-ion cells can extend to 60 °C which poses a safety concern in electric mobility accidental loadings.

In addition, the cell also exhibits a strong strain-rate dependency, mostly attributed to the flow of electrolyte through the active materials [10,13,14]. The electrolyte flow mostly follows a Darcy-type equation, with increasing flow velocity leading to hydrostatic pressure build-up. This strengthens the macroscopic cell response, but it also results in earlier damage of the porous skeleton and progressive crushing [65]. This strain rate dependency comes with a significant shape change of the force-displacement response from a convex-linear curve at low speed to a bi-linear response at high speed, along with a softening at intermediate speeds. This could be attributed to self-heating of the cells, which is not explicitly considered in this study. Due to the limited access to the indented region, it is experimentally challenging to monitor the temperature evolution due to plastic dissipation. Previous studies reported temperature increases of only a few degrees away from the indenter (e.g. [65,79]). To obtain an upper bound of the temperature increase during the intermediate, we assume that the experiment is adiabatic and that the plastic work is fully converted into heat. The temperature evolution is then given by the equality
(19)
ρ
C p
d T =
σ ¯
d
ε
¯
p
,
where ρ = 2500kg/m 3 is the cell density and CP = 1130J/kg/K is the specific heat capacity of a NMC pouch cell [80]. Considering the hardening law identified at a strain rate of 1/s, the temperature increases by 7.2 °C at an equivalent plastic strain of 0.3. According to the temperature dependent hardening model, this temperature increase results in a decrease of the transition stress by 0.6 MPa. On the other hand, comparing the slow and intermediate strain rate stress response, a decrease of 3 MPa is observed. With all the hypotheses considered, full conversion of plastic work into heat, adiabatic process, and neglecting the strain rate hardening, it can be concluded that another mechanism is likely to be involved in the softening of the cell. For example, the additional pressure exerted by the fluid on the porous skeleton could lead to early damage and subsequent softening even in the absence of self-heating. Similarly, the experimental results suggest that the sensitivity of cell mechanical behavior to their state of charge cannot be neglected at slow speed, due to a combined effect of charge-induced swelling and material properties change. As the simulation results are merely affected by charging-related thickness change (Fig. 9), it is concluded that charging affects the effective mechanical properties of Li-ion cells. While the influence of the state of charge on the force evolution is rather limited, it seems to have a more significant role on the force and displacement at the onset of short circuit (see Fig. 6). However, this influence weakens with higher loading rate. Between the fully discharged and charged batteries, the maximum force drops of respectively 13 %, 7 % and 5 % at slow, intermediate, and high speed. This is tentatively explained by the larger influence of the liquid electrolyte in the mechanical response at higher indentation speeds, which is unaffected by the state of charge of the cell.

The open-circuit voltage evolution depends mostly on the temperature and strain-rate. At low temperatures (−5 °C and 0 °C), the voltage drops smoothly after the onset of short circuit, while at higher temperatures (60 °C and 70 °C), the voltage drops within one second from the first voltage drop to the final short circuit. At low speed, the voltage remains constant up to the onset of short circuit while it starts to decrease earlier at intermediate and fast loading speeds. These differences in the shapes of voltage curve are tentatively attributed to a change in failure sequence. During slow experiments, all the individual cell layers are compressed homogeneously, and no significant pressure is added by the electrolyte. On the other hand, at high speed, a significant overpressure is exerted by the liquid electrolyte below the indenter leading to inhomogeneous damage pattern through the cell thickness. This behavior is consistent with micrograph reported in [65], where two distinct failure patterns are observed: (i) shear bands at low speed and (ii) heavy damage of the top layers with the occurrence of many kinks bands at high speed.

A comprehensive experimental campaign was conducted on commercial 4 Ah Li-ion pouch cells, involving hemispherical indentation experiments across a wide range of conditions: temperatures from –5 °C to 70 °C, strain rates from 0.001/s to 1000/s, and states of charge (SoC) from 0.0 to 0.97. The mechanical response of the cells was found to be highly dependent on all these variables. Increasing the temperature leads to a pronounced softening of the mechanical response, resulting in failure at lower loads (43 % reduction from 20 °C to 70 °C) and smaller indentation depths. The influence of strain rate is complex, as it affects multiple meso‑structural deformation mechanisms. While it positively affects the initial yield stress of the jellystack, its impact on the maximum force is non-monotonic. The maximum force is reduced by 15 % from slow to intermediate speed, while a 50 % reduction of the displacement at the onset of short-circuit is observed from slow to high speed indentation. In contrast, the effect of the state of charge is relatively minor, with a maximum variation of 13 % in peak force under otherwise identical conditions.

To capture these observations, we developed a phenomenological model that describes the effective stress–strain response of jellystacks. The model is based on the pressure-dependent Deshpande–Fleck yield criterion with associated plastic flow. Most dependencies are embedded in the hardening law, which accounts for the characteristic transition from convex-up to linear hardening. In addition to rate-dependent terms, the transition strain is explicitly defined as a function of temperature and state of charge. After calibrating the model parameters, finite element simulations were performed and shown to reproduce the experimental results accurately across a wide range of conditions.

Although ongoing research aims to develop physics-based models for the mechanical response of Li-ion batteries, the proposed phenomenological model already provides a practical tool for engineers. It can support the design of safer electronic devices and enhance the crashworthiness of battery-integrated vehicle structures. At the same time, the accurate modeling of the plasticity response will enable the development of a predictive short-circuit initiation model across temperatures, strain rates and states of charge.
